target.title: [AI-assisted] Fix duplicated acp server args in ACP client
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: `openclaw acp client` always prefixes the spawned server command with `acp`, so if an upstream caller also passes `serverArgs=["acp", ...]`, the effective command becomes `openclaw acp acp ...` and fails.
- Why it matters: this makes ACP client startup brittle and causes session creation to fail for callers that already include the subcommand in forwarded args.
- What changed: normalize `serverArgs` in the ACP client by dropping one leading `acp` when present, then prepend the canonical `acp` subcommand exactly once; added a regression test for this case.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no changes to ACP server behavior, permissions, routing, session semantics, or any non-ACP CLI paths.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

ACP client startup is now tolerant of callers that redundantly include `acp` in forwarded `serverArgs`.
Before: `openclaw acp client --server openclaw --server-args acp --server-verbose` could spawn `openclaw acp acp --verbose` and fail.
After: the same invocation normalizes to `openclaw acp --verbose` and proceeds to session creation.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

This changes ACP client command construction only by deduping a redundant leading `acp` token in forwarded server args.
Risk is low because the change narrows an invalid invocation into the canonical one instead of expanding execution surface.
Mitigation: added a regression test covering the duplicated-`acp` case.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local host runtime
- Model/provider: openai-codex/gpt-5.4
- Integration/channel (if any): ACP CLI
- Relevant config (redacted): default local OpenClaw CLI setup; no special ACP config required for repro

### Steps

1. Run `openclaw acp client --server openclaw --server-args acp --server-verbose`
2. Observe the spawned server command
3. Attempt session creation

### Expected

- The ACP client should spawn the canonical server command once as `openclaw acp --verbose`
- Session creation should proceed successfully

### Actual

- Before the fix, this could spawn `openclaw acp acp --verbose`
- That failed with `error: too many arguments for 'acp'`

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Before:

```text
[acp-client] spawning: openclaw acp acp --verbose
error: too many arguments for 'acp'
```

After:

```text
[acp-client] spawning: openclaw acp --verbose
[acp-client] creating session
```

Targeted test:

```text
npx vitest run src/acp/client.test.ts
```

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
    - Reproduced the failing duplicated-acp invocation locally
    - Verified the same invocation now spawns openclaw acp --verbose
    - Verified it reaches session creation successfully
- Ran npx vitest run src/acp/client.test.ts
- Edge cases checked:
    - callers that already pass leading acp in serverArgs
    - existing --server-verbose behavior remains intact after normalization
- What you did **not** verify:
    - full repository green on pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test
    - broader ACP server behavior outside this argument-normalization path

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (Yes)
- Config/env changes? (No)
- Migration needed? (No)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this PR / commit
- Files/config to restore: src/acp/client.ts, src/acp/client.test.ts
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for:
    - ACP client spawning malformed commands
    - regression where valid serverArgs are incorrectly altered

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: overly broad normalization could accidentally rewrite intended server args
    - Mitigation: normalization only removes a single leading acp; all other args are preserved verbatim
- Risk: reviewers may assume the full repository test/build matrix is green
    - Mitigation: this PR explicitly reports targeted verification only; local pnpm build and pnpm test currently fail due to unrelated existing repository issues outside this change

target.title: gateway: enforce 512 KB max length for chat.send message
target.body:
### Summary

Add a **512 KB** (UTF-8 byte length) maximum for the `chat.send` message body in the gateway.  
This makes chat behavior predictable for clients, prevents extremely large messages from putting unnecessary memory and CPU pressure on the gateway, and aligns chat payload limits with other content-safety and size-guard rails in the system.

### Changes

- **Implementation** (`src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts`)
  - Define and export `CHAT_SEND_MESSAGE_MAX_BYTES = 512 * 1024` (512 KB).
  - In `sanitizeChatSendMessageInput`, enforce a UTF-8 byte length check via `Buffer.byteLength(normalized, "utf8")` after NFC normalization and null-byte checks.
  - If the message exceeds 512 KB, return:
    - `{ ok: false, error: "message exceeds maximum length (max 512 KB). Shorten or split your message." }`
  - Otherwise, keep the existing control-character stripping and return `{ ok: true, message }`.

- **Protocol schema** (`src/gateway/protocol/schema/logs-chat.ts`)
  - Add `CHAT_SEND_MESSAGE_MAX_LENGTH = 512 * 1024`.
  - Change `ChatSendParamsSchema.message` from `Type.String()` to:
    - `Type.String({ maxLength: CHAT_SEND_MESSAGE_MAX_LENGTH })`.

- **Tests** (`src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts`)
  - Import `CHAT_SEND_MESSAGE_MAX_BYTES` alongside `sanitizeChatSendMessageInput`.
  - Add tests that:
    - Accept a message at exactly 512 KB (ASCII).
    - Reject a message over 512 KB and assert the error includes `"512"`, `"maximum length"`, and `"Shorten or split"`.
    - Verify enforcement based on UTF-8 byte length using multi-byte characters.

- **Docs** (`docs/gateway/protocol.md`)
  - Add a **Chat methods** subsection stating:
    - `chat.send` message body is limited to **512 KB** (UTF-8 byte length).
    - Oversized messages are rejected and clients should shorten or split them.

### Rationale

- Prevents unbounded `chat.send` payloads from causing excessive memory usage or slow processing on the gateway.
- Gives clients a clear and documented upper bound so they can split or truncate messages proactively.
- Keeps chat behavior consistent with other gateway size and safety limits, reducing surprise for operators and users.

### Testing

- `pnpm test -- src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts --run`
- Lint/format checks via `scripts/committer` (includes `pnpm check`).

### Checklist

- [x] Chat message size limit enforced at both schema and server sanitization layers.
- [x] Limit is based on UTF-8 byte length (multi-byte characters handled correctly).
- [x] Documentation updated to reflect the new limit and its rationale.
- [x] No version bump or release flow changes; commit only (no push).

target.title: feat(feishu): pass through form_value, input_value, option(s) from card actions
target.body:
## Problem

Feishu card action callbacks include more than just `action.value` (button values). They also carry `form_value`, `input_value`, `name`, `option`, and `options` fields for form submissions, input components, and select menus.

Currently, `handleFeishuCardAction` only extracts `action.value`, silently dropping all form data. This makes it impossible for agents to process interactive card form submissions.

Closes #43202

## Changes

1. **Extended `FeishuCardActionEvent` type** — added `input_value`, `name`, `form_value`, `option`, and `options` fields to the `action` object
2. **Form-aware payload construction** in `handleFeishuCardAction`:
   - When form data is present → builds a structured JSON payload including all form fields
   - When no form data (simple button click) → preserves existing behavior exactly

## Backward Compatibility

- Simple button clicks (the only previously supported interaction) behave identically
- No changes to any other files
- The structured JSON payload for form submissions is a new code path that only triggers when form fields are present

target.title: [codex] restore QR bootstrap operator handoff
target.body:
## Summary
- restore QR/setup-code bootstrap handoff so native onboarding receives both the primary node token and a bounded operator token
- keep the QR operator token limited to `operator.approvals`, `operator.read`, and `operator.write`
- document the native mobile contract and cover the gateway/client handoff with focused tests

## Root Cause
PR #81292 / commit `b17e77a22b` changed the built-in setup-code bootstrap profile to node-only and removed the `hello-ok.auth.deviceTokens` handoff. That reversed the mobile onboarding behavior restored by PR #58382 / commit `69fe999373`, where iOS and Android suppress the operator loop during bootstrap auth and only start it after persisting an operator token from the trusted bootstrap handoff.

## Verification
- `pnpm docs:list`
- `git diff --check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/shared/device-bootstrap-profile.test.ts src/infra/device-bootstrap.test.ts src/infra/device-pairing.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server.auth.control-ui.test.ts`
- `swift test --package-path apps/shared/OpenClawKit --filter GatewayNodeSessionTests`
- `CODEX_REVIEW_AUTO_TESTS=0 bash /Users/guti/.codex/skills/codex-review/scripts/codex-review --mode local`

## Real Behavior Proof
Behavior addressed: mobile QR/setup-code onboarding again receives a node token plus a minimally scoped operator token from the trusted bootstrap handoff.
Real environment tested: local Codex worktree on macOS, gateway/bootstrap Vitest suites, shared Swift package tests.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/shared/device-bootstrap-profile.test.ts src/infra/device-bootstrap.test.ts src/infra/device-pairing.test.ts`; `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server.auth.control-ui.test.ts`; `swift test --package-path apps/shared/OpenClawKit --filter GatewayNodeSessionTests`; `git diff --check`; `pnpm docs:list`; `codex-review --mode local`.
Evidence after fix: gateway tests assert `hello-ok.auth.deviceTokens` includes the operator handoff token for QR bootstrap, paired devices have node + operator tokens, allowed operator scopes are bounded, and admin/pairing replay paths still fail.
Observed result after fix: focused gateway/bootstrap tests, Swift handoff tests, docs listing, diff check, and codex-review all passed.
What was not tested: Android Gradle unit tests could not start because this worktree has no configured Android SDK location (`ANDROID_HOME` or `apps/android/local.properties`).

target.title: fix(qa): reduce WhatsApp live RTT setup cost
target.body:
Summary
- Skip WhatsApp live QA agent workspace bootstrap and force exact-marker canaries to run with thinking disabled in both defaults and per-model params.
- Add per-scenario WhatsApp live timings, RSS samples, complete heap snapshot capture, and redacted gateway trace artifacts.
- Skip eager participating-group metadata hydration for DM-only WhatsApp sessions.
- Add changelog coverage for the QA and WhatsApp runtime changes.

Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/qa-lab/src/live-transports/whatsapp/whatsapp-live.runtime.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/qa-lab/src/live-transports/whatsapp/whatsapp-live.runtime.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/live-transports/shared/live-gateway.runtime.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/whatsapp/src/monitor-inbox.behavior.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/qa-lab/src/live-transports/whatsapp/whatsapp-live.runtime.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/monitor-inbox.behavior.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- Testbox-through-Crabbox `tbx_01krx90szx6z16pqh37w0206tf`: scoped `pnpm check:changed -- <touched paths>`, exit 0
- Testbox-through-Crabbox `tbx_01krx9ne1a4egr0qxpp22chkf5`: scoped `pnpm check:changed -- <touched paths>`, exit 0
- Testbox-through-Crabbox `tbx_01krxagz91ep6d1ca29qx5b00q`: scoped `pnpm check:changed -- <touched paths>`, exit 0

Live evidence
- `26027068186` at `fdc2d0e9`: WhatsApp job passed; RTT `7532ms`; trace isolated ~`5141ms` in agent/model before outbound send.
- `26027596921` at `74c4bb73`: WhatsApp job passed; RTT `7930ms`; trace isolated ~`5890ms` in agent/model before outbound send.
- `26028278115` at `9ef7fbe1`: WhatsApp job passed; RTT `10393ms`; trace isolated ~`7129ms` in agent/model before outbound send.

Behavior addressed: WhatsApp live QA canary artifacts now separate channel/provider cost from agent/model cost, capture complete heap snapshots, and avoid unnecessary setup/group hydration in the DM-only path.
Real environment tested: live WhatsApp Convex QA on GitHub Actions, plus Blacksmith Testbox-through-Crabbox changed gates.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `pnpm openclaw qa whatsapp --provider-mode live-frontier --model openai/gpt-5.5 --alt-model openai/gpt-5.5 --scenario whatsapp-canary` through `qa-live-transports-convex.yml`.
Evidence after fix: WhatsApp provider-path trace is now visible; the latest run shows inbound/provider/outbound overhead is small compared with agent/model time.
Observed result after fix: this branch improves observability and removes some setup work, but it does not yet reduce the live canary into the target range; current dominant cost is agent/model pre-outbound latency.
What was not tested: full workflow green is not claimed; the exact workflow runs may show unrelated lane failures after the WhatsApp job/artifact succeeded.

target.title: fix(gateway): rejected config.patch/apply never queues SIGUSR1 restart
target.body:
## Summary

Pins the invariant that a **rejected** `config.patch` / `config.apply` RPC never schedules a gateway SIGUSR1 restart, with both a structural guard and regression tests. Filed in response to a downstream incident where a long-running gateway ended up in a permanent "draining for restart" zombie state after two consecutive rejected `config.patch`/`config.apply` calls.

## Reproduction

Inside a long-running gateway:

1. A control-plane caller invokes `config.patch` to inject `channels.slack.*` tokens.
2. Gateway rejects: `gateway config.patch cannot change protected config paths: channels.slack.botToken, channels.slack.appToken, ...`
3. Caller retries with `config.apply` (full file). Gateway rejects again with the same protected-paths error.
4. **Despite both patches being rejected**, the reload manager logged `config change requires gateway restart (channels.slack) — deferring until N operation(s), R reply(ies), E embedded run(s) complete` and queued two SIGUSR1s ~2s apart.
5. The second SIGUSR1 arrived during the in-process restart window (after `ready` but before `starting channels and sidecars` finished). From that moment forward every model call returned `Gateway is draining for restart; new tasks are not accepted`. State persisted for ~75 minutes until recovery via process restart.

## Diagnosis

Reading the current gateway code, the reload manager already only acts on *successfully-applied* writes:
- The chokidar file-watcher path reads the on-disk snapshot via `readSnapshot()`.
- The in-process write path goes through `notifyRuntimeConfigWriteListeners`, which fires *only* after `replaceConfigFile` commits.

And `config.ts:configHandlers["config.patch"]` and `["config.apply"]` both validate (parseConfigJson5 → applyMergePatch → restoreRedactedValues → validateConfigObjectWithPlugins → ensureResolvableSecretRefsOrRespond → diffConfigPaths) **before** calling `commitGatewayConfigWrite` and `resolveGatewayConfigRestartWriteResult`. Any rejection branch calls `respond(false, ..., errorShape(...))` and returns without writing.

So the structural invariant *holds today* — but it's enforced by control flow alone, not by a structural guard, and the observed correlation between rejected patches and a queued restart was nowhere covered by regression tests. The most likely actual mechanism in the incident is a separate successful `replaceConfigFile` write racing alongside the two rejected ones, which is a legitimate restart trigger but was temporally indistinguishable in the logs from the rejected patches.

This PR locks the rejection-no-restart invariant in place so a future refactor can't break it.

## Changes

### `src/gateway/server-methods/config-write-flow.ts`

`resolveGatewayConfigRestartWriteResult` now short-circuits when `changedPaths.length === 0`:
- Refuses to write the restart sentinel.
- Refuses to call `scheduleGatewaySigusr1Restart`.
- Logs an audit warning with the control-plane actor.

Callers (`config.patch` and `config.apply` handlers in `config.ts`) only invoke this helper after `commitGatewayConfigWrite` succeeds and a non-empty `changedPaths` is computed, so this guard is a defense-in-depth backstop, not a behavior change in any current code path.

### `src/gateway/server.config-patch.test.ts`

New regression suite `gateway rejected config writes do not queue restart` spies on `scheduleGatewaySigusr1Restart` via `vi.spyOn(restartModule, ...)` and asserts no call happens when:

- `config.patch` is rejected for invalid JSON (parse failure)
- `config.patch` is rejected for `raw=null` (non-object)
- `config.patch` is rejected for SecretRef resolution failure
- `config.apply` is rejected for invalid raw (parse failure)
- `config.apply` is rejected for SecretRef resolution failure
- **Two rejected patches arrive consecutively** — direct replay of the incident scenario (config.patch rejected → config.apply rejected, neither queues a restart)

Each test also asserts the on-disk config hash is unchanged across the rejection.

## Acceptance criteria

- [x] Rejected `config.patch`/`config.apply` calls do not trigger reload evaluation or restart queue → covered by 6 regression tests
- [x] Reload manager only acts on successfully-applied config diffs → already enforced (reload reads disk / subscribes to post-commit notifications); now also backed by the `changedPaths.length === 0` guard
- [x] Regression test: rejected protected-path patch → no reload, no restart, gateway state unchanged → covered (the agent-side `assertGatewayConfigMutationAllowed` protected-paths guard is already covered by `src/agents/openclaw-gateway-tool.test.ts`; this PR adds the server-side rejection coverage)
- [ ] **Stretch:** detect & coalesce duplicate SIGUSR1 arriving during the in-process restart window — **not included in this PR**, see Out of scope below

## Test results

```
pnpm vitest --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-server.config.ts
  src/gateway/server.config-patch.test.ts
    gateway rejected config writes do not queue restart
      ✓ does not schedule SIGUSR1 when config.patch is rejected for invalid JSON
      ✓ does not schedule SIGUSR1 when config.patch is rejected for non-object raw
      ✓ does not schedule SIGUSR1 when config.patch is rejected for SecretRef resolution failure
      ✓ does not schedule SIGUSR1 when config.apply is rejected for invalid raw
      ✓ does not schedule SIGUSR1 when config.apply is rejected for SecretRef resolution failure
      ✓ does not schedule SIGUSR1 when two rejected patches arrive consecutively
```

Full `vitest.gateway-server` config: **1045 passing**, 1 pre-existing failure in `gateway config methods > returns noop for config.patch when config is unchanged` (asserts `res.payload?.noop` is `true` but server returns `noop` differently; verified on `main` at the current HEAD). Not introduced by this PR.

Full `vitest.gateway-methods`: **831 passing**.  
Full `vitest.gateway-core`: **1938 passing** (includes all `config-reload.test.ts` and `server-reload-handlers.test.ts`).  
`src/agents/openclaw-gateway-tool.test.ts`: **26 passing × 2 projects** (existing agent-side protected-path coverage unchanged).  
`src/infra/infra-runtime.test.ts`: **27 passing** (existing SIGUSR1 coalescing coverage unchanged).

## Out of scope

**Coalescing duplicate SIGUSR1 arriving during in-process restart startup window** — `scheduleGatewaySigusr1Restart` in `src/infra/restart.ts` already coalesces against `pendingRestartTimer`/`hasUnconsumedRestartSignal`, but `run-loop.ts` resets `shuttingDown=false` when a new lifecycle iteration begins, which is what let the second SIGUSR1 from the original incident race into the new lifecycle's startup window. Fixing this requires deeper changes to the run-loop's signal acceptance window and warrants a separate PR with its own test plan.

target.title: [codex] fix prompt regression from PR #83454
target.body:
## Summary This PR keeps the Codex harness anchor intact while preventing Codex's named native personality overlay from outranking a user-provided OpenClaw `SOUL.md`. It combines five related pieces in one reviewable change: 1. **SOUL-aware Codex personality routing** - Adds `plugins.entries.codex.config.personalityMode`. - Defaults to `"soul_when_present"`. - When OpenClaw injects `SOUL.md` into the Codex turn context, OpenClaw sends Codex app-server `personality: "none"` for that thread. - This is a structured app-server enum, not prompt text that tells the model to "have no personality". The positive personality source remains `SOUL.md`. 2. **A narrow OpenClaw/Codex bridge** - Keeps Codex base/model/project-doc behavior in the native Codex lane. - Adds developer-level bridge wording only when OpenClaw turn context is injected. - Explains that Codex is the native execution harness, OpenClaw is the host runtime, and OpenClaw turn context is reference data rather than developer policy. - When the resolved personality mode disables Codex's native personality, explicitly points personality/tone/agent voice at the SOUL file when compatible with higher-priority safety, privacy, tool, and current-request rules. 3. **Prompt hardening plus docs/tests** - Keeps raw `SOUL.md`, skills, and bootstrap files out of Codex developer instructions. - Projects context-engine `systemPromptAddition` into Codex turn reference context instead of Codex developer instructions. - Documents the priority model, configuration modes, and context-engine behavior. - Adds regression tests for default SOUL behavior, opt-out behavior, bridge wording, session binding, context-engine projection, and type/config parsing. 4. **Lightweight Codex AGENTS.md fix** - Keeps lightweight cron user input byte-for-byte. - Stops sending `project_doc_max_bytes: 0` for lightweight native Codex threads. - This stops OpenClaw from explicitly suppressing Codex's native project-doc loader. Actual `AGENTS.md` ingestion remains Codex app-server behavior. 5. **CI test hardening** - Keeps `source-delivery-plan.test.ts` focused on source-delivery matching by mocking the outbound target normalizer. - Avoids loading the full channel plugin registry in a tiny unit test, which was timing out in the unrelated `checks-node-core-fast` shard. ## Why The previous fix correctly stopped raw OpenClaw workspace prompt files from being promoted into Codex developer instructions. That avoided giving user-editable files developer priority. However, it left an important edge case: if Codex's native personality overlay remained active, a custom OpenClaw `SOUL.md` could still lose personality priority to Codex's built-in friendly/pragmatic personality. That is especially awkward because OpenClaw is not only a coding agent surface, while the Codex harness still needs to preserve its coding-capable anchor and native tool discipline. This PR chooses the middle path: - Keep Codex's native harness identity. - Do not promote raw SOUL text to developer instructions. - Disable Codex's named native personality only when SOUL is present by default. - Use a bridge instruction to say where personality should come from. - Leave explicit config escape hatches for deployments that want Codex personality always on or always off. ## Prior PR Context The preceding prompt-surface work was directionally correct: it separated PI, CLI, ACP, subagent, and Codex app-server prompt lanes so Codex-native turns no longer inherited PI wording, global command hints, or OpenClaw GPT-5 behavior overlays by accident. It also moved OpenClaw workspace bootstrap files out of Codex `developerInstructions`, which avoided the worse failure mode of giving user-editable files developer priority. The adversarial review found that the previous fix created a more subtle priority gap: - Raw `SOUL.md` was no longer over-promoted into developer instructions. - But Codex's own named personality overlay could still sit above the SOUL turn context. - That means a user-provided OpenClaw personality could be present but lose to the native Codex friendly/pragmatic personality. - The bridge existed conceptually, but the PR body did not describe its shape, why it was needed, or why it was preferable to promoting SOUL. This PR is the follow-up that closes that gap while preserving the good part of the previous PR: Codex owns its native harness identity; OpenClaw owns runtime routing and context interpretation; user-editable OpenClaw files remain reference context. ## Adversarial Findings | Severity | Finding | Disposition in this PR | | --- | --- | --- | | 9/10 | Lightweight Codex turns could suppress native `AGENTS.md` while OpenClaw text said Codex loads it. The bad chain was `project_doc_max_bytes: 0` plus skipped OpenClaw bootstrap context. | Fixed. Lightweight cron turns still keep exact user input, and OpenClaw no longer sends the project-doc suppression override. Actual `AGENTS.md` ingestion remains Codex app-server behavior. | | 8/10 | OpenClaw broad behavior/personality overlays can duplicate Codex's native base/model/personality stack. | Kept narrow. Native Codex still avoids the OpenClaw GPT-5 behavior contract in `developerInstructions`; this PR handles the remaining personality conflict with app-server `personalityMode`. | | 7/10 | OpenClaw skills/context can double with Codex native project context or accidentally gain developer priority. | Kept as reference context. Raw workspace files, skills, and context-engine additions stay out of Codex developer policy; the bridge tells Codex how to interpret them. | | 7/10 | Some non-PI prompt surfaces can still receive OpenClaw workflow hints when tools are nonempty. | Not widened here. That belongs to the OpenClaw assembled-prompt surface, not the native Codex app-server lane. This PR keeps only Codex-specific subagent disambiguation in the Codex lane. | ## Fresh Adversarial Audit After the first green run, a fresh read-only audit found two real merge-blocking precision issues and one proof-language overclaim: | Finding | Fix in this PR | | --- | --- | | `personalityMode: "codex"` kept Codex's native personality unset, but the bridge still told the model to use `SOUL.md` for personality. | The SOUL personality pointer now depends on the resolved app-server personality override, not raw SOUL presence. In `"codex"` mode, `SOUL.md` stays turn-input reference context and the bridge does not point personality at it. | | Matching `thread_bootstrap` context-engine resumes skipped old bootstrap history, but also dropped the current turn's `systemPromptAddition`. | Matching bootstrap resumes now omit the old bootstrap transcript while still projecting the current turn's `systemPromptAddition` as quoted reference context, not developer policy. | | The PR proof phrasing implied live proof of Codex actually loading `AGENTS.md`. | The body now says exactly what this PR proves: OpenClaw no longer sends `project_doc_max_bytes: 0`, so it no longer suppresses Codex's native project-doc loader. | ## Bridge Text Shape This PR labels and tests the bridge as a developer-level **OpenClaw/Codex turn-context bridge**. It is intentionally small and has three parts: 1. **Host/runtime disambiguation:** OpenClaw is the host runtime; Codex is the native execution harness. Codex's base identity describes harness capability and tool discipline, not a promise that every OpenClaw turn is coding work. 2. **Authority boundary:** OpenClaw may provide files, memory, skills, and channel context as turn-input reference data. That context helps with task framing and style, but raw files and skills are not developer policy. 3. **SOUL pointer when Codex personality is disabled:** If the resolved personality mode disables Codex's native personality and `SOUL.md` is in the OpenClaw turn context, use it for personality, tone, and agent voice when compatible with higher-priority safety, privacy, tool, and current-request rules. If `personalityMode: "codex"` is configured, `SOUL.md` remains reference context and this personality pointer is omitted. The bridge is developer-level because it is an instruction about **how to rank and interpret** lower-priority OpenClaw context. The SOUL file itself stays lower priority because it is user/workspace-editable content. ## Why Not Promote SOUL We considered the main alternatives: | Option | Result | | --- | --- | | Put raw `SOUL.md` back into `developerInstructions` | Rejected. It would make user-editable personality text developer policy and undo the safety win from the previous PR. | | Leave Codex native personality on and rely on SOUL in turn context | Rejected as the default. It leaves the custom OpenClaw personality below Codex's named friendly/pragmatic overlay, so conflicts resolve in the wrong direction. | | Always disable Codex native personality | Supported via `personalityMode: "none"`, but not the default because some deployments intentionally want stock Codex personality. | | Disable Codex native personality only when SOUL is present, then bridge to SOUL | Chosen. It removes the competing named overlay only when a positive OpenClaw personality source exists. | | Keep Codex native personality even with SOUL | Supported via `personalityMode: "codex"` for operators who prefer Codex's built-in style stack. | The important detail is that `personality: "none"` is not prompt text. It is structured Codex app-server configuration. The model is not told "you have no personality"; instead, the named native overlay is omitted and, when SOUL is the intended personality source, the bridge points to `SOUL.md`. ## Overrides Kept This PR intentionally keeps the native Codex overrides that are actually runtime policy: | Override | Why it stays | | --- | --- | | Visible reply routing for `sourceReplyDeliveryMode === "message_tool_only"` | This is channel/session delivery policy. Automatic mode remains neutral and uses the active Codex delivery path. | | Codex `spawn_agent` primary for Codex subagents | Native Codex subagents should stay native. OpenClaw `sessions_spawn` remains reserved for OpenClaw/ACP delegation. | | Codex base/model instructions | OpenClaw should not override native Codex `baseInstructions`. This PR only uses `developerInstructions` for OpenClaw runtime policy and bridge text. | ## Prompt Priority Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Codex base/model instructions
harness identity, tools, safety"] --> B["Codex native personality enum"] B --> C["OpenClaw developer bridge
runtime + context authority"] C --> D["OpenClaw turn context
SOUL, IDENTITY, USER, MEMORY, skills"] D --> E["Current user request"] S["SOUL.md injected
and personality override active"] --> N["Send app-server personality: none"] N --> B S --> C C --> P["Use SOUL.md for personality/tone
only when Codex personality is disabled"] ``` Important distinction: `personality: "none"` is sent as Codex app-server configuration. It is not inserted into the model prompt as "you have no personality." When the default SOUL path disables Codex's native personality, the model receives a positive developer bridge that names `SOUL.md` as the personality/tone source. ## Context-Engine Hardening Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR CE["context-engine systemPromptAddition"] --> CTX["Codex turn reference context"] CTX --> INPUT["turn/start input"] CE -. "no longer for native Codex" .-> DEV["Codex developerInstructions"] ``` For OpenClaw-assembled prompt surfaces, context engines can still contribute to the assembled system prompt. For native Codex app-server turns, that dynamic guidance is now quoted as turn reference context so retrieval/memory hints do not outrank Codex system/developer policy. ## Configuration New Codex plugin config: ```json5 { "plugins": { "entries": { "codex": { "config": { "personalityMode": "soul_when_present" } } } } } ``` Accepted values: | Value | Behavior | | --- | --- | | `"soul_when_present"` | Default. Disable Codex's named native personality only when OpenClaw injects `SOUL.md`; use SOUL as the personality/tone reference. | | `"codex"` | Keep Codex's native personality overlay even when `SOUL.md` is present. | | `"none"` | Always send Codex `personality: "none"` for app-server threads. | ## Real behavior proof **Behavior addressed:** Native Codex app-server turns now keep Codex harness identity while routing personality priority to OpenClaw `SOUL.md` when SOUL is injected. Context-engine `systemPromptAddition` is no longer appended to Codex developer instructions, and lightweight Codex turns no longer send `project_doc_max_bytes: 0`. **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, built through the real OpenClaw CLI/runtime build path at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-prompt-surface-runtime-separation`, commit `ec96840b1b`. **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ```bash pnpm openclaw --help node --input-type=module --eval ' import { readdirSync, readFileSync } from "node:fs"; const findDistChunk = (needle) => readdirSync("./dist").find((name) => name.endsWith(".js") && readFileSync(`./dist/${name}`, "utf8").includes(needle) ); const configFile = findDistChunk("function resolveCodexPersonalityOverride"); const lifecycleFile = findDistChunk("function buildDeveloperInstructions"); const { s: readCodexPluginConfig, u: resolveCodexPersonalityOverride } = await import(`./dist/${configFile}`); const { i: buildDeveloperInstructions, _: projectContextEngineAssemblyForCodex, o: buildThreadStartParams, } = await import(`./dist/${lifecycleFile}`); const defaultDeveloperInstructions = buildDeveloperInstructions( { sourceReplyDeliveryMode: "automatic" }, { includeOpenClawTurnContextBridge: true, useSoulPromptContextForPersonality: true }, ); const codexModeDeveloperInstructions = buildDeveloperInstructions( { sourceReplyDeliveryMode: "automatic" }, { includeOpenClawTurnContextBridge: true, useSoulPromptContextForPersonality: false }, ); const projection = projectContextEngineAssemblyForCodex({ assembledMessages: [], originalHistoryMessages: [], prompt: "hello from real smoke", systemPromptAddition: "context-engine system", systemPromptAdditionMode: "context", }); const lightweightStart = buildThreadStartParams( { provider: "openai", modelId: "gpt-5.4-codex", authProfileStore: { version: 1, profiles: {} }, bootstrapContextMode: "lightweight", bootstrapContextRunKind: "cron", sourceReplyDeliveryMode: "automatic", }, { cwd: process.cwd(), dynamicTools: [], appServer: { approvalPolicy: "on-request", approvalsReviewer: "user", sandbox: "workspace-write", }, developerInstructions: "lightweight proof", }, ); console.log(JSON.stringify({ openclawCheckout: process.cwd(), commit: "ec96840b1b", distChunks: { configFile, lifecycleFile }, defaultSoulPersonality: resolveCodexPersonalityOverride({ pluginConfig: readCodexPluginConfig({}), hasSoulPromptContext: true, }), explicitNonePersonality: resolveCodexPersonalityOverride({ pluginConfig: readCodexPluginConfig({ personalityMode: "none" }), hasSoulPromptContext: false, }), codexModePersonality: resolveCodexPersonalityOverride({ pluginConfig: readCodexPluginConfig({ personalityMode: "codex" }), hasSoulPromptContext: true, }) ?? null, defaultBridgeMentionsHostRuntime: defaultDeveloperInstructions.includes("OpenClaw is the host runtime"), defaultBridgePointsToSoul: defaultDeveloperInstructions.includes("use SOUL.md from the OpenClaw turn context"), codexModeBridgeMentionsHostRuntime: codexModeDeveloperInstructions.includes("OpenClaw is the host runtime"), codexModeBridgePointsToSoul: codexModeDeveloperInstructions.includes("use SOUL.md from the OpenClaw turn context"), rawSoulTextInDeveloperInstructions: defaultDeveloperInstructions.includes("Soul voice goes here."), contextEngineDeveloperAddition: projection.developerInstructionAddition ?? null, contextEnginePromptHasReferenceHeader: projection.promptText.includes("Context-engine guidance:"), contextEnginePromptHasSystemAddition: projection.promptText.includes("context-engine system"), lightweightProjectDocMaxBytes: lightweightStart.config?.project_doc_max_bytes ?? null, lightweightProjectDocSuppressesAgents: lightweightStart.config?.project_doc_max_bytes === 0, }, null, 2)); ' ``` **Evidence after fix:** Copied live terminal output from the built OpenClaw checkout: ```text OpenClaw 2026.5.17 (ec96840) — All your chats, one OpenClaw. { "openclawCheckout": "/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-prompt-surface-runtime-separation", "commit": "ec96840b1b", "distChunks": { "configFile": "config-Om76xpjW.js", "lifecycleFile": "thread-lifecycle-CYnqtUYN.js" }, "defaultSoulPersonality": "none", "explicitNonePersonality": "none", "codexModePersonality": null, "defaultBridgeMentionsHostRuntime": true, "defaultBridgePointsToSoul": true, "codexModeBridgeMentionsHostRuntime": true, "codexModeBridgePointsToSoul": false, "rawSoulTextInDeveloperInstructions": false, "contextEngineDeveloperAddition": null, "contextEnginePromptHasReferenceHeader": true, "contextEnginePromptHasSystemAddition": true, "lightweightProjectDocMaxBytes": null, "lightweightProjectDocSuppressesAgents": false } ``` **Observed result after fix:** The real built OpenClaw checkout reports the default SOUL path resolves to Codex `personality: "none"`, the `"codex"` config path leaves native personality unset, developer bridge text is present and points to SOUL only for the default SOUL override path, raw SOUL contents are absent from developer instructions, context-engine guidance is present in turn reference context while developer-instruction addition is `null`, and the lightweight Codex path does not suppress native project-doc loading by sending `project_doc_max_bytes: 0`. **What was not tested:** No live paid model request or remote Codex app-server network turn was run for this PR. The live proof exercises the built OpenClaw runtime/prompt-construction path locally; request-construction behavior is covered by focused app-server tests. ## Behavior Addressed Before: - Raw `SOUL.md` was not in developer instructions, which was good. - But Codex's named native personality overlay could still remain active above the SOUL turn context. - Context-engine `systemPromptAddition` could be appended to Codex developer instructions. - Lightweight Codex turns sent `project_doc_max_bytes: 0`, which suppressed native `AGENTS.md` loading while the OpenClaw-side bootstrap was also skipped. After: - Raw SOUL text remains turn context, not developer policy. - The developer bridge says how to interpret OpenClaw context and when to use SOUL for voice. - Default Codex personality is neutralized only when SOUL is present, while `personalityMode: "codex"` keeps the native personality overlay and omits the SOUL personality pointer. - Context-engine dynamic additions are turn reference context for native Codex. - Matching `thread_bootstrap` resumes keep current-turn context-engine guidance as reference context without reprojecting old bootstrap history. - Lightweight Codex turns preserve exact user input without disabling native Codex project docs. ## Real Environment Tested Checkout: `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-prompt-surface-runtime-separation` Branch: `codex/soul-personality-bridge` Commit: `ec96840b1b [codex] route native personality through soul context` ## Exact Steps Or Commands Run After This Patch ```bash git diff --check OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/config.test.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/session-binding.test.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/context-engine-projection.test.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.context-engine.test.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \ src/infra/outbound/source-delivery-plan.test.ts pnpm check:test-types ``` ## Evidence After Fix - Focused Vitest: `7 passed (7)` files, `295 passed (295)` tests. - Source delivery plan test is included in the focused Vitest command; local duration for that file after hardening was `279ms` in the pre-rebase run. - Type test check: `pnpm check:test-types` passed. - Whitespace check: `git diff --check` passed. ## Observed Result After Fix - Codex app-server thread start includes `personality: "none"` when SOUL is injected under the default config. - `personalityMode: "codex"` leaves native Codex personality unset even when SOUL is present. - Developer instructions contain the OpenClaw/Codex bridge and no raw SOUL contents; `personalityMode: "codex"` omits the SOUL personality pointer. - Cron/lightweight bootstrap path does not add the OpenClaw context bridge or personality override, and no longer disables native Codex project docs. - Context-engine `systemPromptAddition` is absent from Codex developer instructions and present in turn reference context, including matching `thread_bootstrap` resume turns. ## What Was Not Tested - No full local repo-wide suite was run; this was intentionally kept to focused local validation plus type checks under the Lexar resource policy. - No live Codex app-server manual smoke was run in this PR turn; the change is covered by the app-server request-construction and run-attempt tests.

target.title: Add Claude ignore rules for generated assets
target.body:
## Summary

- add a repo-level `.claudeignore` so Claude Code skips generated output, dependency/build directories, lockfiles, screenshots, and bulky media assets during normal code navigation
- keep source, docs, tests, and scoped `AGENTS.md` / `CLAUDE.md` files visible
- include a short note that ignored assets remain in git and can still be requested by exact path when needed

## Verification

- `git diff --cached --check`
- `python3 ../context-os/python/agent_cost_leak_check.py --repo . --json`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: OpenClaw had repo-level `AGENTS.md` / `CLAUDE.md` guidance but no repo-level `.claudeignore`, so Claude Code could still include generated output, lockfiles, screenshots, and bulky media assets during normal repo exploration.

Real environment tested: local shallow clone of `openclaw/openclaw` on branch `add-claudeignore-cost-controls`, using a stdlib local scanner from `sravan27/context-os`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `git diff --cached --check` and `python3 ../context-os/python/agent_cost_leak_check.py --repo . --json`.

Evidence after fix: copied live terminal output from the after-fix scanner run:

```json
{
  "score": 34,
  "files": 17934,
  "source": 15413,
  "ai": 3,
  "findings": [
    "Large tracked blobs increase read risk",
    "Large source tree needs a repo map",
    "Multi-language repo shape"
  ]
}
```

Before this patch, the same scanner reported score `52/100` and included `No agent ignore file found`. After this patch, that finding is gone and `ai_config_hits` increases from `2` to `3`.

Observed result after fix: `.claudeignore` is present at repo root and covers dependency/build output, package locks, generated docs/localization baselines, screenshots/reports, common binary media assets, and local secret/operator state.

What was not tested: runtime behavior, package builds, and broad CI suites. This is a repo metadata change only; no application source code or lockfile content changed.

target.title: fix(browser): allow upload from inbound media directory
target.body:
`browser.upload` rejected managed WebChat attachments because upload validation only accepted `DEFAULT_UPLOAD_DIR`. This PR keeps that uploads root and adds the managed inbound media root, including the model-visible `media://inbound/<id>` and `media/inbound/<id>` forms used by WebChat attachment staging.

Fixes #83544

AI-assisted: yes. Codex generated the patch; I reviewed the diff and verified the upload path validation behavior locally.

## Summary

- **Problem:** WebChat attachments are stored as managed inbound media, but `browser.upload` only accepted paths under the temp uploads directory.
- **Why it matters:** An agent that receives a WebChat file attachment can be unable to upload that file into a browser file input.
- **What changed:** Browser upload path validation now uses shared helpers that validate each requested file independently against the uploads directory and then the managed inbound media directory. The helpers also resolve `media://inbound/<id>` and sandbox-relative `media/inbound/<id>` before root validation.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** Arbitrary local paths are still rejected. Managed media references are limited to a single first-level inbound media id, and the final resolved paths still go through the existing root validators.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #83544
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: `browser.upload` can now resolve managed inbound media supplied as an absolute inbound path, `media://inbound/<id>`, or sandbox-relative `media/inbound/<id>`, while rejecting paths outside both allowed roots. Mixed multi-file uploads can combine temp upload files and inbound media files in one request.
- Real environment tested: macOS local OpenClaw checkout, Node v24, branch `fix/browser-upload-inbound-media`, using the browser extension upload path validators and Playwright upload entry tests.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/browser/src/browser/paths.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-tools-core.interactions.set-input-files.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-tools-core.upload-paths.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-tools-core.screenshots-element-selector.test.ts --reporter=verbose

OPENCLAW_HEAVY_CHECK_LOCK_SCOPE=worktree node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo
```

- Evidence after fix:

```text
✓ resolveExistingUploadPaths > resolves canonical inbound media URI references before root validation
✓ resolveExistingUploadPaths > resolves sandbox-relative inbound media paths before root validation
✓ resolveExistingUploadPaths > accepts mixed upload-root and inbound-media files in one request
✓ resolveExistingUploadPaths > rejects nested inbound media URI references
✓ resolveStrictExistingUploadPaths > resolves inbound media URI references for use-time upload validation
✓ resolveStrictExistingUploadPaths > resolves sandbox-relative inbound media paths for use-time upload validation
✓ resolveStrictExistingUploadPaths > accepts mixed upload-root and inbound-media files at use time
✓ armFileUploadViaPlaywright upload path validation > sets files using resolved inbound media paths

Test Files 5 passed (5)
Tests 90 passed (90)
```

- Observed result after fix: Model-visible WebChat media references are normalized to the managed inbound media directory before upload root validation. Each requested file is validated independently, so mixed uploads containing both temp upload files and inbound media files pass when every file belongs to one allowed root. Files outside uploads and inbound media still fail validation; nested/escaped media references are rejected.
- What was not tested: Full live WebChat/Gateway/browser session with a real user attachment. The local proof covers the exact browser upload validation helpers and both Playwright upload entry points that gate the file input behavior.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): ClawSweeper review identified that `media://inbound/<id>` and `media/inbound/<id>` still failed because the previous helper retried only the raw requested path under the physical inbound directory, and later identified mixed-root multi-file validation as missing.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: The upload action originally hardcoded the temp uploads directory. The first fix added the physical inbound media directory but did not resolve the managed media reference forms WebChat exposes to agents, and validated the whole file list against one root at a time.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Existing upload tests mocked path validation and did not cover model-visible WebChat media references, mixed-root multi-file uploads, or the file chooser use-time validation path.
- Contributing context (if known): Browser upload, WebChat attachment storage, and sandbox media staging are separate surfaces with different path forms.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/browser/src/browser/paths.test.ts`, `extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-tools-core.upload-paths.test.ts`, `extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-tools-core.interactions.set-input-files.test.ts`, `extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Upload validation accepts managed inbound media references and sandbox-relative media paths, supports mixed uploads from both allowed roots, rejects nested media refs, rejects paths outside both roots, and revalidates at Playwright use-time.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: These tests exercise the shared upload resolver and both browser file-input entry points without requiring a live Gateway session.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None before this PR.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

WebChat file attachments can be passed to `browser.upload` using the managed inbound media references that agents see in prompts, not only hidden host absolute paths. Multi-file uploads can combine normal temp upload files with managed inbound media files.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[WebChat attachment] -> media://inbound/report.pdf -> browser.upload -> uploads root check -> reject
[mixed files] -> [uploads/a.txt, media://inbound/b.txt] -> one-root batch validation -> reject

After:
[WebChat attachment] -> media://inbound/report.pdf -> managed inbound resolver -> inbound root validation -> upload
[WebChat attachment] -> media/inbound/report.pdf -> managed inbound resolver -> inbound root validation -> upload
[mixed files] -> per-file root validation -> upload
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? Yes
- If any Yes, explain risk + mitigation: The upload allowlist now includes managed inbound media in addition to the existing uploads directory. The mitigation is narrow parsing: only `media://inbound/<single-id>`, `media/inbound/<single-id>`, and files that validate inside the managed inbound directory are accepted. Nested references, path traversal, symlinks, hardlinks, and files outside both roots remain rejected by the existing validators.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node v24, local OpenClaw checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): WebChat attachment path forms -> browser upload validation
- Relevant config (redacted): Default OpenClaw config; no secrets used

### Steps

1. Create test files under fixture upload and inbound media directories.
2. Resolve upload paths for `media://inbound/report.pdf`, `media/inbound/report.pdf`, and a mixed request containing both upload-root and inbound-media files through `resolveExistingUploadPaths` and `resolveStrictExistingUploadPaths`.
3. Exercise the `browser.upload`, `setInputFilesViaPlaywright`, and `armFileUploadViaPlaywright` upload validation paths.

### Expected

- Managed inbound media references resolve to the inbound media file and pass upload validation.
- Mixed upload-root and inbound-media file lists pass when every path belongs to one allowed root.
- Nested media references and paths outside both allowed roots are rejected.

### Actual

- Matches expected in the focused local tests: 90/90 passed.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: uploads root fallback, inbound absolute path fallback, `media://inbound/<id>`, `media/inbound/<id>`, mixed upload-root plus inbound-media file lists, nested media reference rejection, outside-root rejection, file chooser use-time validation, direct `setInputFiles` validation.
- Edge cases checked: nested encoded URI path (`media://inbound/nested%2Fsecret.pdf`), missing file rejection in strict validation, outside path rejection.
- What you did **not** verify: A full live WebChat attachment flow through Gateway into a real browser file input.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Browser upload can now select files from managed inbound media.
  - Mitigation: Only OpenClaw-managed inbound media references and files passing existing root validation are accepted; arbitrary local paths remain rejected.

Generated with Codex.

target.title: fix(models): label picker auth via effective provider order
target.body:
## Summary
- Pass the effective OpenAI/Codex accepted auth provider set into the shared /models provider label path.
- Keep the change display-only: model selection and dispatch routing are unchanged.
- Add regression coverage for an OpenAI picker page that should display the Codex OAuth profile instead of falling back to OPENAI_API_KEY.

Fixes #83574.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior or issue addressed: The `/models` provider header for `openai` should label the effective Codex OAuth auth profile selected by `auth.order.openai`, instead of implying an API-key fallback such as `api-key (env: OPENAI_API_KEY)`.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, Node v25.9.0, with the real `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-models.ts` implementation and a real temporary `auth-profiles.json` store under `$TMP_HOME/.openclaw/agents/main/agent`. The store contained only `openai-codex:user@example.com` as an OAuth profile; there was no `openai:default` API-key profile.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the real header formatter through `node --import tsx` with `provider: "openai"`, `auth.order.openai: ["openai-codex:user@example.com"]`, and `models.providers.openai.baseUrl: "https://api.openai.com/v1"`.

```bash
TMP_HOME=$(mktemp -d)
AGENT_DIR="$TMP_HOME/.openclaw/agents/main/agent"
mkdir -p "$AGENT_DIR"
HOME="$TMP_HOME" AGENT_DIR="$AGENT_DIR" node --import tsx --eval '
  import { formatModelsAvailableHeader } from "./src/auto-reply/reply/commands-models.ts";
  const cfg = {
    auth: { order: { openai: ["openai-codex:user@example.com"] } },
    models: { providers: { openai: { baseUrl: "https://api.openai.com/v1" } } }
  };
  console.log(formatModelsAvailableHeader({
    provider: "openai",
    total: 2,
    cfg,
    agentId: "main",
    agentDir: process.env.AGENT_DIR,
    workspaceDir: process.cwd(),
  }));
'
```

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the real OpenClaw formatter path:

```text
Models (openai · 🔑 oauth (openai-codex:user@example.com)) — 2 available
```

Observed result after fix: The provider picker header now shows `oauth (openai-codex:user@example.com)` for `openai`, proving the display follows the effective Codex OAuth auth path and does not fall back to `api-key (env: OPENAI_API_KEY)` for this configuration.

Not tested: Live Telegram button rendering was not exercised; the Telegram callback uses this same shared `formatModelsAvailableHeader` path, and the change is covered at that shared layer.

## Verification
- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/commands-models.test.ts
- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/model-auth-label.test.ts
- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/commands-status.test.ts
- pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/auto-reply/reply/commands-models.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-models.test.ts src/agents/model-auth-label.test.ts
- pnpm exec oxlint src/auto-reply/reply/commands-models.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-models.test.ts src/agents/model-auth-label.test.ts

commands.author: Takhoffman

target.title: fix(ui): prevent reading indicator from sticking after assistant response
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: After the assistant finishes responding in Control UI webchat, a reading indicator bubble (three animated dots) persists indefinitely below the last message as a separate bubble.
- Why it matters: Users see a stuck typing animation on every message, requiring a manual page refresh to clear it. This is a consistent UX regression that affects all webchat users.
- What changed: Removed the `canAbort` fallback in the chat render stream assignment (`displayStream = props.stream ?? (canAbort ? "" : null)` -> `displayStream = props.stream ?? null`). Updated tests to match new behavior.
- What did NOT change: Run lifecycle logic, session management, WebSocket event handling. The fix is purely in the render path and does not touch the runId mismatch path that the previous PR (#36943) modified.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #83513
- Related #36911, #36943
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Reading indicator bubble (three animated dots) persists as a separate bubble below every assistant response in Control UI webchat, never disappearing until page refresh.

- **Real environment tested**: OpenClaw v2026.5.16-beta.6 (be2d60d) on Ubuntu 24.04 LTS, running via x86_64 PVE VM (tailscale IP 100.101.182.60), accessed through nginx reverse proxy to port 443 with ZeroSSL certificate. Browser: Chrome 148 on Windows 10.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. Apply patch to compiled JS bundle: `sed -i 's/v=e.stream??(r?``:null)/v=e.stream??null/' dist/control-ui/assets/index-8PSUymZb.js`
  2. Restart gateway: `systemctl --user restart openclaw-gateway`
  3. Open Control UI webchat URL `https://vm100.sfunds.cn/?token=REDACTED` in Chrome browser
  4. Hard-refresh (Ctrl+F5) to bypass service worker cache
  5. Send 5 test messages to the assistant and observe the reading indicator behavior

- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal capture of the patch step and restart command:
  ```
  $ sed -i 's/v=e.stream??(r?``:null)/v=e.stream??null/' dist/control-ui/assets/index-8PSUymZb.js
  $ systemctl --user restart openclaw-gateway
  $ systemctl --user is-active openclaw-gateway
  active
  ```
  After applying the patch and restarting the gateway, sent 5 consecutive messages via the Control UI webchat at `https://vm100.sfunds.cn/?token=REDACTED`. The reading indicator appeared normally during assistant thinking, then correctly disappeared after the assistant response was rendered. The UI no longer displays a stuck `...` bubble below completed responses. Each of the 5 test messages resulted in clean single-bubble responses. This was observed directly in the browser over a 3-minute test session.

- **Observed result after fix**: Reading indicator appears during assistant thinking (expected UX), disappears immediately after the response bubble is delivered to the UI. No extra `...` bubble remains below any response. Each message produces exactly one response bubble with no leftover typing indicator. Before the fix, a secondary `...` bubble would appear and never go away. After the fix, the issue is completely eliminated across all 5 test messages.

- **What was not tested**: Mobile browser webchat (tested on desktop Chrome only), other model providers (only tested with minimax/MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed deepseek fallbacks), WebSocket reconnection mid-stream, model fallback with different runId, multi-session scenarios, long-running conversations exceeding 50 messages.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: In `ui/src/ui/views/chat.ts` line 1006, the stream variable is assigned with a fallback that recreates an empty stream when `canAbort` is true. After the `chat` final event, `chatStream` is properly cleared to `null` by `Yv()`. However `canAbort` stays true because the cached session snapshot still shows `hasActiveRun: true`. Sessions are never reloaded in the Chat tab (`xV()` returns false when `tab === "chat"`), so the fallback converts `null` back into `""`, which the reading-indicator renderer (`gj`) treats as an active empty stream and permanently renders the dots bubble.

- Missing detection / guardrail: The render function should not recreate a fake empty stream after the real stream has been cleaned.

- Contributing context: A previous fix attempt (#36943) tried to solve this in the mismatched-runId path of `zx()` by clearing `chatRunId`, but risked interrupting genuinely active runs. This fix takes a simpler approach by removing only the render-time fallback.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [x] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: When `stream` is null and `canAbort` is true, no reading-indicator should render (loading skeleton instead). When `stream` is `""`, reading-indicator should render.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Unit test at the rendering level directly covers the stream null path.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Updated existing test. Added new test for empty-stream case.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None. The only change is eliminating the stuck reading indicator.

## Diagram

```
Before: send message -> assistant thinks (reading indicator) -> response arrives -> reading indicator stays forever
After:  send message -> assistant thinks (reading indicator) -> response arrives -> reading indicator gone
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Ubuntu 24.04 LTS
- Runtime/container: OpenClaw v2026.5.16-beta.6 (be2d60d)
- Model/provider: minimax/MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed
- Integration/channel: Control UI webchat

### Steps

1. Open Control UI webchat in browser
2. Send any message
3. Wait for assistant to respond

### Expected

Response message displayed, no reading-indicator after response.

### Actual

(Before fix) Reading-indicator dots bubble persists below response.
(After fix) No extra bubble.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Live terminal capture (patch command + restart + verification)

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios: 5+ consecutive messages via Control UI webchat on patched production bundle
- Edge cases checked: Rapid message send, empty message (rejected client-side), message with image attachments
- What I did **not** verify: WebSocket reconnect mid-stream, model fallback (different runId), mobile viewport

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Removing the fallback might delay initial reading indicator appearance.
  - Mitigation: Reading indicator is shown by a separate mechanism when `props.stream` is `""` (set by the send function). The fallback only kept it alive after cleanup, not created it initially.

target.title: fix: recover stale subagent completion announces
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: stale requester wake delivery can leave completed subagent announces with no visible direct-agent reply.
- Why it matters: completed child work may not surface to the requester, while the gateway emits repeated direct-announce errors.
- What changed: completion direct delivery now retries requester wake without transcript-commit waiting when that mode is unsupported, and if the wake then proves stale it forces a message-tool handoff instead of accepting an empty direct reply.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no channel/provider-specific policy, no raw external send fallback, and no compatibility shim for old announce APIs.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #83699
- Related #82803
- Related #82804
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: stale subagent completion direct announce after `transcript_commit_wait_unsupported` followed by `no_active_run` no longer stops at `completion agent did not produce a visible reply`; it forces message-tool completion delivery.
- Real environment tested: Windows local worktree, Node v24.15.0, OpenClaw agent delivery seam with the observed stale queue outcomes reproduced in the test harness.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node node_modules\vitest\vitest.mjs run src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts --reporter=dot`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Terminal capture:

```text
RUN  v4.1.6 C:/OpenClaw/worktrees/bug-020-subagent-direct-announce

Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests  104 passed (104)
Start at  09:53:13
Duration  27.06s (transform 10.93s, setup 907ms, import 13.61s, tests 12.05s, environment 0ms)
```

- Observed result after fix: the new regression covers a stale wake sequence of `transcript_commit_wait_unsupported` then `no_active_run`, an empty primary direct-agent response, and a successful forced message-tool handoff with idempotency key suffix `:message-tool`.
- What was not tested: live gateway/provider rerun against the private affected scheduled workflow was not run; local `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ...` was blocked by local `spawn EPERM`, Blacksmith Testbox was unavailable because `blacksmith` is not installed, and direct AWS Crabbox was blocked by missing AWS credentials. The equivalent direct Vitest command completed the touched seam proof locally.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Redacted runtime log excerpt from the observed 2026-05-18 UTC window:

```text
announce_total=131 unique_runs=44
queue_fail_by_reason={"transcript_commit_wait_unsupported":277,"no_active_run":131}
queue message failed: sessionId=[redacted requester session id] reason=transcript_commit_wait_unsupported
queue message failed: sessionId=[redacted requester session id] reason=no_active_run
Subagent completion direct announce failed for run [redacted run id]: completion agent did not produce a visible reply
```

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: completion direct delivery treated an unsupported transcript-commit wake plus stale requester run as a normal direct-agent fallback, then failed when that direct-agent call returned no visible payload.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no regression for the stale wake sequence that still has a completed child result available for mediated delivery.
- Contributing context (if known): the prior related attempt in #82804 closed without merging, leaving #82803 stale relative to current mainline behavior.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: transcript-commit wake unsupported, best-effort wake reports no active run, primary direct-agent response is empty, forced message-tool handoff succeeds.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug is in the subagent announce delivery seam before provider generation and before channel-specific transport.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none for this stale wake plus empty direct response recovery path.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Completed subagent announces are more likely to surface back to the requester when the original requester run is stale. If the forced message-tool handoff also fails, the error now reports message-tool delivery failure instead of only no visible direct reply.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
subagent completion -> transcript-wait wake unsupported/no active -> direct agent empty -> logged failure

After:
subagent completion -> retry wake without transcript wait -> stale run detected -> forced message-tool handoff -> requester-visible completion
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Windows local worktree
- Runtime/container: Node v24.15.0
- Model/provider: not required for seam proof; observed production model/provider was not available in local evidence
- Integration/channel (if any): Slack-style delivery route in regression coverage; bug is channel-agnostic subagent announce orchestration
- Relevant config (redacted): queue debounce defaults preserved; private session/run identifiers redacted

### Steps

1. Simulate a subagent completion where requester wake with `waitForTranscriptCommit` returns `transcript_commit_wait_unsupported`.
2. Simulate the best-effort wake retry returning `no_active_run`.
3. Simulate an empty primary direct-agent response and then message-tool evidence from the forced handoff.

### Expected

- The delivery result is `delivered: true`, `path: direct`, and the second agent call uses `sourceReplyDeliveryMode: message_tool_only` with an idempotency key suffix.

### Actual

- Before this patch, the same class of delivery ended as `completion agent did not produce a visible reply` in the observed logs.
- After this patch, the regression passes and the full touched test file reports 104 passing tests.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: full `src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts` after patch, including the new stale wake regression.
- Edge cases checked: existing empty direct-response tests now verify the forced message-tool failure path instead of raw external fallback; configured message-tool media/channel cases remain covered by the same file.
- What you did **not** verify: live private gateway workflow replay, because it requires private sessions/credentials and exact affected scheduled workflow state.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: forcing a second handoff could duplicate an announce if an empty primary response actually delivered externally without evidence.
  - Mitigation: the forced path is limited to the stale wake pattern and requires message-tool delivery evidence; idempotency key suffix keeps the fallback distinct and auditable.
